cards.back: Any innovation that includes a program as an integral part of its function. Can be physical (e.g. self-driving car), nonphysical software (e.g. photo editing app), or nonphysical concept (e.g. e-commerce).

cards.front: How does collaboration improve computing innovations?
cards.back: Effective collaboration produces an innovation that reflects the diversity of talents and perspectives of those who designed it. Diverse perspectives help avoid bias. Consultation with users helps develop a program that fully incorporates their perspectives.
A collaboration model where one programmer writes code while another observes and provides real-time feedback. Both roles actively contribute — the observer catches errors the writer might miss.

cards.front: Four Interpersonal Skills for Effective Collaboration
cards.back: Communication, consensus building, conflict resolution, and negotiation.
To solve problems or to pursue interests through creative expression. Understanding the purpose gives developers an improved ability to build the innovation.
A collection of program statements that performs a specific task when run by a computer. Needs to work for a variety of inputs and situations.
A programming style where program statements execute when triggered by an event rather than through sequential flow of control. An event is an action that supplies input data to a program.

Iterative: continuously refines the whole project based on feedback — earlier phases may be revisited.
Incremental: builds and verifies one piece at a time before adding the next.
Investigating and reflecting, designing, prototyping, and testing. The process can be ordered and intentional, or exploratory in nature.
A document that defines the requirements for a program. The design phase outlines how to accomplish the specification.
A written description of the function of a code segment, event, procedure, or program and how it was developed. Should be written throughout development, not just at the end.
A form of program documentation written into the program to be read by people. Do not affect how the program runs. Not all programming environments support comments.

Logic error: program runs but produces wrong results.
Syntax error: language rules are broken, program cannot run.
Run-time error: occurs during execution.
Overflow error: number is outside the defined range of values.

cards.front: Five Ways to Find and Correct Errors
cards.back: Test cases, hand tracing, visualizations, debuggers, and adding extra output statements.
Using defined inputs to ensure an algorithm or program produces expected outcomes. Test inputs should demonstrate different expected outcomes at or just beyond the extremes of input data.
Any code developed collaboratively or borrowed from another source must be acknowledged in the program documentation, including the origin or original author's name.

cards.front: What does the design phase of a program include?
cards.back: Brainstorming, planning and storyboarding, organizing into modules and functional components, creating UI layout diagrams, and developing a testing strategy.

cards.front: Ways to investigate user needs during development
cards.back: Collecting data through surveys, user testing, interviews, and direct observations.

Input: data sent to a computer for processing. Can be tactile, audio, visual, or text. Can come from a user or other programs.
Output: any data sent from a program to a device. Usually based on the program's input or prior state.

cards.front: Why is bias a concern in computing innovations?
cards.back: Computing innovations can embed bias when the development team lacks diverse perspectives. Bias can disadvantage certain users. Including diverse collaborators and consulting users helps identify and avoid bias.
